perturbation.original_text: Subject to the prior subscription rights of the above­listed parties, the Holding Company may offer for sale in a direct community offering (the "Community Offering" and when referred to together with or subsequent to the Subscription Offering, the "Subscription and Community Offering") the Shares not subscribed for or ordered in the Subscription Offering to members of the general public to whom a copy of the Prospectus (as hereinafter defined) is delivered with a preference given first to natural persons and trusts of natural persons residing in the local community as defined in the Plan, and thereafter to cover orders of other members of the general public.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to the prior subscription rights of the above­listed parties, the Holding Company may offer for sale in a direct community offering (the "Community Offering" and when referred to together with or subsequent to the Subscription Offering, the "Subscription and Community Offering") the Shares not subscribed for or ordered in the Subscription Offering to any persons, regardless of their location or connection to the Holding Company, without delivering a copy of the Prospectus.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement to deliver a prospectus and allows sales to any person, contradicting securities laws regarding required disclosures.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933 requires that a prospectus be delivered to investors before or at the time of sale. Removing this requirement violates federal law.

Prohibitions relating to interstate commerce and the mails (a) Sale or delivery after sale of unregistered securities Unless a registration statement is in effect as to a security, it shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly- (1) to make use of any means or instruments of transportation or communication in interstate commerce or
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts securities laws by removing the requirement to deliver a prospectus, as stated in the perturbation explanation and law explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 77e, concerns 'Prohibitions relating to interstate commerce and the mails' and mentions 'sale or delivery after sale of unregistered securities,' implying a regulatory framework for securities offerings which the changed text undermines by eliminating the prospectus requirement. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the 'Securities Act of 1933' and lists 15 USC 77e, further supporting the relevance of prospectus requirements. The modification essentially creates a scenario where securities can be offered without mandatory disclosures, a clear violation, thus 'YES'.

perturbation.original_text: The Holding Company will maintain appropriate arrangements for depositing all funds received from persons mailing or delivering subscriptions for or orders to purchase Shares in the Offering with the Bank, on an interest-bearing basis at the rate described in the Prospectus until the Closing Date and satisfaction of all conditions precedent to the release of Shares in the Offering in accordance with the Plan and as described in the Prospectus or until refunds of such funds have been made to the persons entitled thereto or withdrawal authorizations canceled in accordance with the Plan and as described in the Prospectus. The Holding Company will maintain such records of all funds received to permit the funds of each subscriber to be separately insured by the FDIC (to the maximum extent allowable) and to enable the Holding Company to make the appropriate refunds of such funds in the event that such refunds are required to be made in accordance with the Plan and as described in the Prospectus.
perturbation.changed_text: The Holding Company will deposit all funds received from persons for subscriptions for Shares in a non-interest-bearing account with a financial institution chosen at the Holding Company’s discretion. The Holding Company will maintain records necessary only for its internal accounting purposes, and the funds of each subscriber will not be separately insured by the FDIC.
perturbation.explanation: This contradicts requirements regarding the handling of subscription funds, including the provision of interest and FDIC insurance.
perturbation.law_explanation: This regulation dictates the requirements for FDIC insurance coverage, including the need for adequate record-keeping to determine insurance coverage. The modified text removes the requirement for separate insurance, thus violating FDIC regulations.

perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that funds received for subscriptions would be deposited in an interest-bearing account and separately insured by the FDIC. The changed text directly contradicts this by stating the funds will be in a non-interest-bearing account and will not be separately insured by the FDIC, which violates FDIC regulations, as explained by the provided Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, although incomplete, mentions 'Accounts held by an agent' which is relevant to holding subscription funds, indicating the necessity for proper record keeping and insurance coverage determination. The modified text eliminates the separate insurance and associated record-keeping, creating a contradiction because it actively reverses the stipulations outlined in the original agreement concerning financial handling and insurance of subscriber funds.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York without regard to principles of conflicts of law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Principality of Sealand, irrespective of its enforceability.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying the Principality of Sealand, which is not a recognized sovereign state, as the governing law creates uncertainty and potential unenforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UCC generally requires a reasonable relationship between the jurisdiction chosen and the transaction. Choosing the laws of a non-recognized entity might violate this principle, leading to questions about enforceability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), [the Uniform Commercial Code] applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of [the Uniform Commercial Code] specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) Section 2-402 ; (2) Sections 2A-105 and 2A-106 ; (3) Section 4-102 ; (4) Section 4A-507 ; (5) Section 5-116 ; [(6) Section 6-103 ; (7) Section 8-110 ; (8) Sections 9-301 through 9 - 307 . perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies the laws of New York, while the changed contract specifies the laws of the Principality of Sealand. The Law Explanation suggests that the UCC requires a reasonable relationship between the jurisdiction chosen and the transaction, and choosing the laws of a non-recognized entity might violate this principle. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from the Uniform Commercial Code § 1-301, states that parties may agree that the law of another state or nation shall govern their rights and duties when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to that state or nation. Since Sealand is not a recognized sovereign state, specifying it as the governing law introduces uncertainty and potential unenforceability, thus contradicting the requirement for a reasonable relationship as implied in the UCC.
